---
title: "Research"
description: "Research in spatial and computational epidemiology: reading disease risk from imperfect surveillance data, with Bayesian spatiotemporal methods and reproducible tooling."
toc: false
---

::: {.lede}
My research is about reading disease risk off of imperfect surveillance data.
:::

Most of what we know about where and when infectious and vector-borne disease is
circulating comes from systems built for detection, not inference: traps and
tests placed where people expect to find something, effort that varies from one
county and year to the next, and outcomes recorded at whatever resolution the
reporting allows. I work on getting honest, well-calibrated estimates out of
data like that.

## How I approach it

Two commitments run through the work. The first is taking the data-generating
process seriously: where and how hard someone looked shapes what turns up, so
surveillance effort and detection belong inside the model, not in a limitations
paragraph. The second is validating a method before trusting it. New spatial
estimators get checked against reference implementations, and new models get run
on simulated data with a known answer before they touch a real outcome. I work
mostly in Bayesian hierarchical and spatiotemporal models (CAR/BYM2 spatial
structures, distributed-lag climate terms, spatial-confounding adjustments),
because they let me borrow strength across space and time while staying explicit
about uncertainty.

## Current work

My MPH thesis studies West Nile virus mosquito surveillance across California,
modeling infection rate in mosquito pools against environmental covariates and
surveillance effort on a county-month grid. Because the surveillance data is
access-restricted, I built the validation before the analysis: a simulation
framework that measures whether each candidate model recovers a known effect at
its stated uncertainty. It already showed that leaving out seasonal and
month-to-month structure badly under-covers the intervals, something the point
estimates alone would hide. [More on the thesis.](projects/wnv-thesis.qmd)

Earlier work runs along the same line. A sole-authored spatial analysis of
COVID-19 mortality and its socioeconomic determinants across 3,000-plus U.S.
counties (SaTScan clustering with spatial lag and error regression), and cluster
analyses of Lyme disease and Chagas risk, all come back to the same question of
how to find real spatial signal in observational health data.
[Publications.](publications.qmd)

## Where I want to take it

I want to do doctoral research in spatial and computational epidemiology:
methods and pipelines that make surveillance data usable for inference, with an
emphasis on vector-borne and environmentally mediated disease and on the
disparities that surface when you look at risk spatially. I'm particularly
interested in preferential-sampling and detection-bias problems, spatiotemporal
models that borrow strength without smoothing away the signal, and open,
reproducible tools that other public-health groups can actually run.
